name: Maternal haematocrit at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 'Clinical Physiology in Obstetrics': as a consequence of disproportionate plasma-volume expansion vs red-cell-mass expansion, venous haematocrit drops from a non-pregnant mean of ~40% to ~33% by the third trimester. This is the canonical 'physiological anaemia of pregnancy' figure, replicated across multiple subsequent cohort studies and standard obstetric texts.

extraction.method: triangulated from Hytten 1980 + secondary obstetric references

notes: Packed red-cell fraction at term, after physiological haemodilution.

name: Maternal haemoglobin concentration at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: term haemoglobin ~11.5 g/dL reflects the haemodilution-driven fall from a non-pregnant baseline of ~13.5 g/dL. WHO defines anaemia in pregnancy as Hb <11.0 g/dL; the dataset value sits just above this threshold, consistent with the lower end of the normal-physiological range at term in iron-replete women.

extraction.method: triangulated from Hytten 1980 + WHO obstetric guidelines

notes: Whole-blood haemoglobin at term. Caveats: WHO defines anaemia in pregnancy as Hb < 11 g/dL; entries below that threshold should be marked accordingly.

name: Hill coefficient for maternal oxygen-haemoglobin binding
tier_rationale: Tier A: Hill coefficient ~2.7 for adult human haemoglobin is the canonical textbook value, derivable from Severinghaus 1979 (PMID 35496) equation 1, the form S = ((PO2^3 + 150*PO2)^-1 * 23400 + 1)^-1, where the cubic numerator enforces a Hill exponent of approximately 2.7 at half-saturation. Triangulated across multiple secondary sources including the Dash & Bassingthwaighte 2010 modernised equations (PMID 20162361) and contemporary blood-gas references.

extraction.method: triangulated from Severinghaus 1979 equation form + secondary literature consensus

applicability.population: Adult human blood at standard pH and temperature

notes: Hill exponent characterising cooperativity of adult haemoglobin oxygen binding, used in the O2-Hb dissociation relationship. Caveats: Value is for adult haemoglobin (HbA); does not describe fetal haemoglobin (HbF).

name: P50 for maternal oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation
tier_rationale: Tier A verified directly against Severinghaus 1979 J Appl Physiol (PMID 35496): the standard P50 = 26.6 mmHg appears as the explicit constant in equation 4 of Severinghaus's published equations for human adult blood O2-Hb dissociation. The canonical textbook reference.
notes: Partial pressure of oxygen at which 50% of adult haemoglobin is saturated, at standard pH and temperature. Caveats: Shifts with pH (Bohr effect), temperature, and 2,3-BPG concentration; the value here is at standard conditions.

name: Bohr coefficient (Δlog P50 per ΔpH)
tier_rationale: Tier A: Bohr coefficient (dlog P50 / dpH) = -0.48 at standard physiological conditions is the canonical reference value for adult human haemoglobin, derivable from Severinghaus 1979 equation 4 ((PO2/26.6)^0.184 - 2.2 form) and used in the Dash & Bassingthwaighte 2010 modernised model. Documented in StatPearls 'Physiology, Bohr Effect' (NBK526028) and across multiple clinical-physiology references.

extraction.method: triangulated from Severinghaus 1979 + Dash 2010 + clinical references

notes: Bohr coefficient (dlog P50 / dpH). Verified at the level of paper relevance (Dash 2010 is the canonical modern reference). The specific -0.48 value is the textbook canonical value; Dash 2010 derives this behaviour from its Hill-type equations rather than printing the coefficient itself in the abstract.

name: Maternal plasma volume in early pregnancy
tier_rationale: Tier B verified directly against Bernstein, Ziegler & Badger 2001 Obstetrics & Gynecology (PMID 11339913): prospective Evans-blue dye-dilution study, n=10 women followed from preconception. Reports 2320 ± 280 mL plasma volume at 12 menstrual weeks of pregnancy (= 2.32 ± 0.28 L). Expansion of ~14% over follicular-phase baseline (~2.04 L).
notes: First-trimester plasma volume. Bernstein 2001 measured at LH surge + 70 days (= 12 menstrual weeks). Confirmation of the actual ramp-up of plasma volume in early gestation.

name: Maternal plasma volume at term
tier_rationale: Tier B verified directly against de Haas et al. 2017 Ultrasound in Obstetrics & Gynecology (PMID 28169502) meta-analysis of 30 studies (1934-2007). Pooled maximum plasma-volume increase in third trimester is 1.13 L (95% CI 1.07-1.19), representing a 45.6% rise over the non-pregnant reference. The 1.13 L term increase is the best-supported magnitude in the literature.
notes: Plasma volume at term in healthy singleton pregnancies; risen ~50% above non-pregnant baseline.

name: Maternal red-cell mass at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: red-cell mass expands by ~250 mL above the non-pregnant baseline of ~1.4 L without iron supplementation (and ~400-450 mL with iron). The dataset's 1.65 L term value matches the iron-unsupplemented case. Replicated across multiple cohorts; canonical in obstetric haematology references.

extraction.method: triangulated from Hytten 1980 + obstetric haematology consensus

notes: Total red-cell volume at term; increase lags plasma volume expansion, producing the physiological haemodilution of pregnancy.

name: Maternal total blood volume
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: total maternal blood volume at term ~5.6 L reflects ~3.85 L plasma volume (plasma_volume_l) + ~1.65 L red-cell mass (red_cell_mass_l), matching the canonical 30-50% above non-pregnant baseline (~4.0 L) widely cited in obstetric physiology references.

extraction.method: derived sum of plasma + red-cell mass; matches Hytten 1980 net rise

notes: Total circulating blood volume, expanded above the non-pregnant baseline of approximately 4.5 L. Caveats: Volume rises throughout pregnancy; this entry summarises the term value. Trimester-resolved entries should be added when finer data is wired in.

name: Plasma fibrinogen at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 'Clinical Physiology in Obstetrics': fibrinogen rises from a non-pregnant baseline of ~3 g/L to ~5-6 g/L at term, part of the pregnancy-induced hypercoagulable state. Replicated across multiple obstetric haematology references including GLOWM 'Hematological Normal Ranges in Pregnancy'.
notes: Fibrinogen nearly doubles in normal pregnancy. Combined with other coagulation-factor rises, pregnancy is a hypercoagulable state (~5-6× baseline VTE risk).

name: Platelet count at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: platelet count drops modestly through pregnancy due to haemodilution + increased consumption. Term mean ~220 ×10^9/L vs non-pregnant 250-300. Gestational thrombocytopenia (defined as count <150) occurs in ~7-10% of normal pregnancies and is benign.

name: Haemoglobin concentration, pre-pregnancy baseline
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: pre-pregnancy haemoglobin in healthy women of reproductive age is approximately 12.5-14.5 g/dL. Acts as the anchor for the term Hb already in the dataset (~11.5 g/dL) — the fall reflects plasma-volume expansion outpacing red-cell-mass expansion (physiological haemodilution of pregnancy).

applicability.population: Healthy non-pregnant women of reproductive age

name: Mean corpuscular volume at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: mean corpuscular volume rises modestly through pregnancy from a non-pregnant baseline of ~87 fL to ~92 fL at term, reflecting the higher reticulocyte fraction in the expanded red-cell mass. Microcytosis (MCV <80) in late pregnancy is a sensitive indicator of iron-deficient erythropoiesis even without overt anaemia.

source_check.source_citation: Chandra et al. 2012, Indian J Hematol Blood Transfus (PMC3422383)
source_check.quote: there is a small increase in mean corpuscular volume (MCV), of an average of 4 fl in an iron-replete woman
source_check.extracted_value: Chandra 2012 states MCV rises by an average of ~4 fL in iron-replete pregnancy. From a non-pregnant baseline of ~87-88 fL this gives a term MCV around 91-92 fL, consistent with the stored 92 fL term value; the absolute term figure is not stated.

notes: Pairs with ferritin_term_ng_per_ml: MCV <80 + ferritin <12 is the classical iron-deficiency picture.

name: Serum total protein at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: serum total protein falls from a non-pregnant baseline of ~7.0 g/dL to ~6.0 g/dL by term — the fall is driven primarily by the albumin decrement (already in the dataset) with smaller contributions from the globulin fractions. The drop in oncotic pressure contributes to the physiological oedema of pregnancy.

source_check.source_citation: Perinatology.com Pregnancy Reference Values, Total Protein (citing Abbassi-Ghanavati 2009 / Williams Obstetrics)
source_check.extracted_value: The perinatology.com reference table (citing Abbassi-Ghanavati 2009 Obstet Gynecol and Williams Obstetrics) gives a third-trimester total-protein range of 5.6-6.7 g/dL. The stored term central of 6.0 g/dL falls inside this range.

notes: Coupled to serum_albumin_term_g_per_dl already in the dataset; the albumin fall accounts for most of the total-protein decrement.

name: Plasma fibrinogen, pre-pregnancy baseline
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: pre-pregnancy plasma fibrinogen in healthy women of reproductive age is approximately 2.5-3.5 g/L. Acts as the anchor for the term fibrinogen value already in the dataset (~5.5 g/L) — fibrinogen approximately doubles by term as part of the hypercoagulable adaptation of pregnancy.

source_check.quote: Plasma concentrations typically range between 200 and 400 mg/dL.
source_check.extracted_value: StatPearls gives the normal (non-pregnant) adult plasma fibrinogen range as 200-400 mg/dL, i.e. 2.0-4.0 g/L. The stored pre-pregnancy baseline of 3.0 g/L sits at the centre of this range.

applicability.population: Healthy non-pregnant women of reproductive age

notes: Pairs with fibrinogen_term_g_per_l for trajectory modelling; doubling is one of the most robust haemostatic shifts in pregnancy.

name: White blood cell count at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 and standard obstetric haematology: WBC count rises through pregnancy from non-pregnant ~6-7 x10^9/L to ~10-11 x10^9/L at term, driven primarily by neutrophilia. Counts up to ~16 x10^9/L can be physiological in late pregnancy; further elevation during labour is well-documented. Lymphocyte count is essentially unchanged.

source_check.source_citation: GLOWM, Hematological Normal Ranges in Pregnancy (Table 1)
source_check.extracted_value: GLOWM's sample third-trimester WBC reference interval is 6-16 x10^9/L. The stored term central of 10.5 x10^9/L falls inside this interval.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, third trimester (not in labour)

name: Serum albumin at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: serum albumin falls from a non-pregnant baseline of ~4.0 g/dL to ~3.0 g/dL at term, primarily due to plasma-volume expansion outpacing synthesis (haemodilution). The fall lowers colloid osmotic pressure modestly and contributes to physiological oedema of pregnancy.

source_check.quote: Research has indicated that in healthy pregnancies, serum albumin concentrations decline from a mean of 42 g/L in nonpregnant women to 31 g/L near the end of pregnancy due to an increase in plasma volume
source_check.extracted_value: The review states serum albumin declines from a mean of 42 g/L in non-pregnant women to 31 g/L near the end of pregnancy. 31 g/L = 3.1 g/dL, essentially matching the stored term central of 3.0 g/dL (stored range 2.8-3.4).

name: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 and standard obstetric haematology: ESR rises dramatically through pregnancy, driven by the large rise in plasma fibrinogen and other acute-phase reactants together with haemodilution. Term values typically 30-90 mm/h vs non-pregnant <20; the wide range reflects technique (Westergren) and the strong dependence on plasma protein composition. ESR is therefore non-specific as an inflammatory marker in pregnancy.

source_check.source_citation: GLOWM, Hematological Normal Ranges in Pregnancy (Table 1)
source_check.extracted_value: GLOWM's sample third-trimester reference interval for ESR is 13-70 mm/h (median 32). The stored term central of 50 mm/h falls inside this interval; the source's median is lower than 50.

notes: Westergren method. ESR is not useful as an inflammatory marker in pregnancy because the physiological rise overwhelms the inflammatory signal.

name: Serum iron at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: serum iron falls through pregnancy from a non-pregnant baseline of ~80-100 µg/dL to ~30-90 µg/dL at term, reflecting net iron transfer to the fetus and expansion of maternal red-cell mass. The wide range reflects the strong dependence on dietary iron and supplementation status — values below 30 µg/dL without supplementation are common but not strictly pathological.

source_check.source_citation: Yang et al. 2019, Ann Clin Biochem
source_check.quote: In the third trimester, the reference intervals for serum ferritin, serum iron, total iron-binding capacity and transferrin saturation are 7.2–122.2 mg/L, 5.83–21.52 µmol/L, 49.40–122.76 µmol/L and 8.22–52.75%, respectively.
source_check.extracted_value: Yang 2019 reports a third-trimester serum-iron reference interval of 5.83-21.52 umol/L in pregnant women, which converts to ~32.6-120.2 ug/dL (x5.585). The stored term central of 60 ug/dL falls inside this interval.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, third trimester, dietary iron intake uncontrolled

notes: Diurnal variation and supplementation status make a single serum-iron value unreliable in isolation; ferritin is the standard storage-iron marker.

name: Serum ferritin at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 and modern obstetric haematology: serum ferritin falls substantially through pregnancy from a non-pregnant baseline of ~30-100 ng/mL to ~5-35 ng/mL at term as storage iron is mobilised for the maternal red-cell mass expansion and fetal iron stores. Term ferritin <12 ng/mL indicates iron-deficient erythropoiesis even without overt anaemia.

source_check.source_citation: Yang et al. 2019, Ann Clin Biochem
source_check.quote: In the third trimester, the reference intervals for serum ferritin, serum iron, total iron-binding capacity and transferrin saturation are 7.2–122.2 mg/L, 5.83–21.52 µmol/L, 49.40–122.76 µmol/L and 8.22–52.75%, respectively.
source_check.extracted_value: Yang 2019 reports a third-trimester serum-ferritin reference interval of 7.2-122.2 mg/L (ug/L, equivalent to ng/mL) in pregnant women. The stored term central of 15 ng/mL falls inside this interval. GLOWM's pregnancy table gives a similar 5-110 ng/ml third-trimester range.

notes: Ferritin <12 ng/mL is the WHO threshold for depleted iron stores; nearly half of un-supplemented term pregnancies cross it.

name: Fetal microchimeric cell concentration in maternal blood, first trimester baseline
tier_rationale: Tier D (hypothesis-only): fetal cell trafficking into maternal circulation begins in early pregnancy but quantitative concentrations are highly variable and assay-dependent. Bianchi 1996 documents the phenomenon but exact uptake/clearance kinetics remain open. DO NOT USE FOR PREDICTION.
notes: Hypothesis-only Tier D parameter used in maternal_microchimerism_trajectory submodel.

name: Fetal microchimeric cell concentration in maternal blood at term
tier_rationale: Tier D (hypothesis-only): term concentrations of fetal microchimeric cells in maternal blood reported across orders of magnitude (Bianchi 1996 and successors). The central value here is illustrative — true distributions span <0.1 to >10 cells/mL across cohorts and methods. DO NOT USE FOR PREDICTION.
notes: Hypothesis-only Tier D parameter.

name: Plasma D-dimer at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Kline et al. 2005 Clin Chem: D-dimer rises monotonically across normal pregnancy, with third-trimester medians around 1.5 μg/mL (~3× the conventional non-pregnant VTE-exclusion threshold of 0.5 μg/mL). Replicated across multiple obstetric haematology series; the Kline data are widely cited as the basis for revised pregnancy-specific D-dimer thresholds.

source_check.source_citation: Siennicka et al. 2020, BioMed Res Int (PMC7273490)
source_check.quote: Reference value ranges for D-D without adjustment for the entire group for successive trimesters were 167-721, 298-1653, and 483-2256 ng/mL.
source_check.extracted_value: Siennicka 2020 reports an unadjusted third-trimester D-dimer reference interval of 483-2256 ng/mL (= 0.483-2.256 ug/mL) in physiological pregnancy. The stored term central of 1.5 ug/mL falls inside this interval.

notes: Distribution is broad and right-skewed; the non-pregnant VTE-exclusion threshold (0.5 μg/mL) is not useful in pregnancy.

name: Transferrin saturation at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 'Clinical Physiology in Obstetrics': iron demands of pregnancy outstrip absorption in most women, so transferrin saturation falls from a non-pregnant ~30–35% to ~20% at term in the absence of iron supplementation. Replicated in modern obstetric haematology references.

source_check.source_citation: Yang et al. 2019, Ann Clin Biochem
source_check.quote: In the third trimester, the reference intervals for serum ferritin, serum iron, total iron-binding capacity and transferrin saturation are 7.2–122.2 mg/L, 5.83–21.52 µmol/L, 49.40–122.76 µmol/L and 8.22–52.75%, respectively.
source_check.extracted_value: Yang 2019 reports a third-trimester transferrin-saturation reference interval of 8.22-52.75% in pregnant women. The stored term central of 20% falls inside this interval.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, third trimester, no iron supplementation

notes: Routine iron supplementation in pregnancy attenuates the fall; values below ~15% are typically classified as iron-deficient.

name: Serum folate at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: serum folate falls modestly through pregnancy (haemodilution + active placental transfer + rising fetal demand). Term values in unsupplemented women centre ~5 ng/mL; deficient values <3 ng/mL raise risk of macrocytic anaemia. Routine periconceptional folate supplementation now blunts the fall.
notes: Modern values are higher in populations with mandatory folate fortification of grain products.

name: Serum vitamin B12 at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Hytten & Chamberlain 1980: serum B12 falls ~30% through pregnancy, predominantly as a consequence of haemodilution rather than true tissue depletion (holotranscobalamin and methylmalonic acid often remain normal). The serum value at term is conventionally lower-bounded at ~150 pg/mL in healthy pregnancy; lower values warrant follow-up.

source_check.source_citation: GLOWM, Hematological Normal Ranges in Pregnancy (Table 1)
source_check.extracted_value: GLOWM's sample third-trimester total vitamin B12 reference interval is 60-390 pmol/L (median 170), which converts to ~81-529 pg/mL (1 pg/mL = 0.738 pmol/L). The stored term central of 250 pg/mL falls inside this interval.

notes: Serum B12 alone misclassifies pregnancy as deficient; second-line metabolic markers are more specific.

name: Coagulation factor IX activity at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Stirling et al. 1984 Thromb Haemost longitudinal haemostasis-in-pregnancy series: factor IX activity rises modestly (~30%) over baseline by term, contributing to the pregnancy hypercoagulable state alongside the larger factor VII/VIII/fibrinogen rises. Replicated across modern pregnancy-coagulation series.
notes: Expressed as % of mean normal pooled non-pregnant adult plasma activity.

name: Coagulation factor X activity at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Stirling et al. 1984 Thromb Haemost: factor X activity rises ~45% above non-pregnant baseline by term. Together with factor VII (~70% rise) and factor IX (~30% rise) this drives the pregnancy-induced procoagulant shift; PT is therefore shortened modestly in normal pregnancy.
notes: Expressed as % of mean normal pooled non-pregnant adult plasma activity.

name: Coagulation factor XII activity at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Stirling et al. 1984 Thromb Haemost: contact-pathway factor XII rises ~60% above non-pregnant baseline by term, paralleling the broader procoagulant shift. Less clinically central than the VII/VIII/X/fibrinogen rises but consistent across pregnancy-haemostasis longitudinal series.
notes: Expressed as % of mean normal pooled non-pregnant adult plasma activity.

name: Free protein S activity at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Faught et al. 1995 Am J Obstet Gynecol longitudinal cohort: free protein S falls substantially through pregnancy (~50% by term, occasionally lower) under oestrogen-driven C4b-binding-protein increase. This is the dominant anticoagulant-system change in pregnancy and contributes meaningfully to the increased VTE risk. Replicated across modern obstetric haemostasis series.
notes: Pregnancy reference ranges for free protein S are substantially lower than the non-pregnant range; many laboratories report a pregnancy-specific lower limit.

name: Protein C activity at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Faught et al. 1995 Am J Obstet Gynecol: protein C activity is essentially unchanged in normal pregnancy (~100%). The pregnancy-induced anticoagulant deficit therefore arises primarily through the protein-S fall rather than a protein-C fall.

source_check.quote: There was no statistically significant change in antigenic or functional protein C levels during normal pregnancy.
source_check.extracted_value: Faught 1995 found no statistically significant change in protein C during normal pregnancy, consistent with the stored ~100% (unchanged) term value.

notes: Expressed as % of mean normal pooled non-pregnant adult plasma activity.

name: Antithrombin activity at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Faught et al. 1995 Am J Obstet Gynecol and the broader pregnancy-haemostasis literature: antithrombin activity is essentially unchanged in normal pregnancy (~95% of non-pregnant baseline). Significant fall (<60%) suggests acquired AT consumption (DIC, severe pre-eclampsia) rather than physiology.
notes: Antithrombin is the headline non-protein-S anticoagulant; preserved AT plus depressed free protein S is the typical pregnancy fingerprint.

name: Plasminogen activator inhibitor 1 at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Kruithof et al. 1987 Blood longitudinal cohort: PAI-1 rises several-fold from non-pregnant baseline (~15 ng/mL) to ~80 ng/mL at term, contributing to the suppressed fibrinolytic capacity that complements the rise in procoagulant factors. Variability is large and assay-method-dependent.
notes: PAI-1 is hepatic/endothelial; the late-pregnancy rise is part of the haemostatic shift that prepares for placental separation at delivery.

name: Plasminogen activator inhibitor 2 at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Kruithof et al. 1987 Blood: PAI-2 is essentially placenta-derived and rises from near-zero pre-pregnancy to ~250 ng/mL at term, tracking placental mass. The rise is so steep that PAI-2 has been proposed as a placental-function biomarker. Wide between-individual variability and assay sensitivity make Tier C the honest call.

source_check.quote: Its level, quantified with a radioimmunoassay, increased from below the detection limit (approximately 10 ng/mL) in normal plasma to 260 ng/mL at term.
source_check.extracted_value: Kruithof 1987 reports PAI-2 rose from below the detection limit (~10 ng/mL) to 260 ng/mL at term, consistent with the stored central 250 ng/mL (within rounding and the stored range 150-400).

notes: PAI-2 falls rapidly post-delivery as the placenta is removed — direct evidence of placental origin.
